1:
"And when he had opened the seventh seal, there was silence in heaven about the space of half an hour." The witnesses note the opening of the seventh seal and the half-hour silence in heaven. Victorinus notes that the silence in heaven signifies the beginning of everlasting rest, described as partial because the silence is interrupted and the order taken up again in the trumpets that follow. The witnesses note John seeing the Lamb open the seventh and last seal, followed by a silence in heaven about the space of half an hour; the solemn hush at the opening of the last seal — that John records the opening of the final seal: "when he had opened the seventh seal, there was silence in heaven about the space of half an hour"; the witnesses note the meaning of this strange silence — the seventh seal opens not with a loud vision but with a pause, "silence in heaven," a solemn hush of about "half an hour"; the commentators read it variously — some, with Victorinus, as a foretaste of the everlasting rest of God's people, others as a pause of solemn expectation and awe before the trumpet-judgments that the seventh seal contains, the whole host of heaven still and waiting as the next great series of God's dealings is about to be unfolded; so that the last seal opens upon a reverent silence, the calm before the sounding of the trumpets; there was silence in heaven about the space of half an hour. The witnesses note when he had opened the seventh seal. The witnesses note there was silence in heaven about the space of half an hour.

The witnesses note the seventh seal opening upon a solemn silence in heaven — read as a foretaste of everlasting rest, or a pause of awe and expectation before the trumpet-judgments that follow.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn silence at the seventh seal. The lesson the expositors draw is that the last seal opens upon a reverent hush, the calm before the sounding of the trumpets. The witnesses note the seventh seal: "when he had opened the seventh seal" — the Lamb opens the last seal; the seventh seal; the the last; the the Lamb; the opens; the final. The expositors observe the silence: "there was silence in heaven" — a solemn hush; the silence; the the hush; the solemn; the the pause; the still. The witnesses press the half an hour: "about the space of half an hour" — a brief, measured pause; the half an hour; the brief; the measured; the the pause; the space. The expositors present the readings: the witnesses note the commentators read it variously — some, with Victorinus, as a foretaste of everlasting rest, others as a pause of solemn expectation before the trumpets. The witnesses observe the the calm before: the calm before the sounding of the trumpets; the the calm before; the the trumpets; the the sounding; the the pause; the awe. The expositors note the the host waiting: the whole host of heaven still and waiting; the the host waiting; the the host; the still; the waiting; the the trumpets. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when he had opened the seventh seal, the there was silence in heaven about the space of half an hour, and the solemn silence. The verse thus records the solemn silence at the seventh seal — teaching that the last seal opens upon a reverent hush, the calm before the trumpets.
2:
"And I saw the seven angels which stood before God; and to them were given seven trumpets." The witnesses note the seven angels before God receiving seven trumpets. Gill notes these are not the seven spirits of God, whose names and office differ, nor the ministers of the word, but the angelic spirits who stand before God ready to do his will. The witnesses note John seeing the seven angels that stand before God, to whom were given seven trumpets; the seven angels armed with trumpets to sound forth God's judgments — that, after the silence, John sees the instruments of the next series of judgments made ready: "the seven angels which stood before God; and to them were given seven trumpets"; the witnesses note the dignity and the office of these angels — they are the seven angels "which stood before God" (the chief of the heavenly host, standing in his immediate presence, ready for his service) — and "to them were given seven trumpets"; the witnesses note that the trumpet, in Scripture, is the instrument of summons, alarm, and the proclaiming of God's acts; so that these seven angels are appointed and armed, each with a trumpet, to sound forth in turn the great series of judgments contained in the seventh seal; and to them were given seven trumpets. The witnesses note I saw the seven angels which stood before God. The witnesses note and to them were given seven trumpets.

The witnesses note the seven angels standing before God, given seven trumpets — the chief of the heavenly host, armed each with a trumpet to sound forth in turn the series of God's judgments.

So the witnesses make the verse the seven angels armed with trumpets. The lesson the expositors draw is that the seven angels who stand before God are given trumpets to sound forth his judgments in turn. The witnesses note the seven angels: "I saw the seven angels which stood before God" — Gill notes the angelic spirits before God; the seven angels; the angelic spirits; the before God; the the host; the stand. The expositors observe the stood before God: standing in his immediate presence; the stood before God; the his presence; the ready; the his service; the the chief. The witnesses press the seven trumpets: "to them were given seven trumpets" — armed with trumpets; the seven trumpets; the armed; the trumpets; the given; the each. The expositors draw the lesson on the the trumpet: the trumpet is the instrument of summons and alarm; the the trumpet; the summons; the alarm; the proclaiming; the God's acts. The witnesses observe the to sound in turn: appointed to sound in turn; the to sound in turn; the appointed; the in turn; the the series; the the judgments. The expositors note the the seventh seal: the judgments contained in the seventh seal; the the seventh seal; the the judgments; the contained; the the series; the unfolded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I saw the seven angels which stood before God, the and to them were given seven trumpets, and the angels armed. The verse thus records the seven angels armed with trumpets — teaching that the seven angels who stand before God are given trumpets to sound forth his judgments in turn.
3:
"And another angel came and stood at the altar, having a golden censer; and there was given unto him much incense, that he should offer it with the prayers of all saints upon the golden altar which was before the throne." The witnesses note the angel at the altar offering incense with the prayers of the saints. Gill notes this is not a created angel, as Gabriel, nor a mere man, but rather Christ the great High Priest, who offers the prayers of the saints, perfumed with the much incense of his own merit and mediation. The witnesses note John seeing another angel stand at the altar with a golden censer, given much incense to offer with the prayers of all saints on the golden altar before the throne; the angel-priest offering the saints' prayers with much incense before God — that, before the trumpets sound, John sees a scene of priestly intercession: "another angel came and stood at the altar, having a golden censer; and there was given unto him much incense, that he should offer it with the prayers of all saints upon the golden altar which was before the throne"; the witnesses note the reading many give — that this angel-priest is Christ himself, or one acting in his office, presenting "the prayers of all saints" before God; and the "much incense" given him is the fragrance of Christ's own merit and mediation, by which the prayers of the saints, in themselves imperfect, are made acceptable and sweet before the throne; so that the saints' prayers ascend to God not in their own worth but perfumed with the incense of the great Intercessor; he should offer it with the prayers of all saints upon the golden altar which was before the throne. The witnesses note another angel came and stood at the altar, having a golden censer. The witnesses note that he should offer it with the prayers of all saints upon the golden altar which was before the throne.

The witnesses note the angel-priest at the altar offering the saints' prayers with much incense — read by many as Christ, the incense of his merit making the saints' imperfect prayers acceptable before God.

So the witnesses make the verse the saints' prayers offered with incense before God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the saints' prayers ascend acceptable to God, perfumed with the incense of Christ's merit and mediation. The witnesses note the at the altar: "another angel came and stood at the altar" — Gill notes read by many as Christ the High Priest; the at the altar; the Christ; the the High Priest; the the angel; the the altar. The expositors observe the golden censer: "having a golden censer" — the censer for the incense; the golden censer; the the censer; the for incense; the golden; the the priest. The witnesses press the much incense: "there was given unto him much incense" — the fragrance of Christ's merit; the much incense; the the fragrance; the Christ's merit; the mediation; the given. The expositors draw the lesson on the prayers of saints: "with the prayers of all saints" — presenting the saints' prayers; the prayers of saints; the the saints; the presented; the offered; the before God. The expositors note the the golden altar: "upon the golden altar which was before the throne" — on the altar before God; the the golden altar; the before the throne; the offered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that he should offer it with the prayers of all saints upon the golden altar, and the angel-priest. The verse thus records the saints' prayers offered with incense — teaching that the saints' prayers ascend acceptable, perfumed with the incense of Christ's merit.
4:
"And the smoke of the incense, which came with the prayers of the saints, ascended up before God out of the angel's hand." The witnesses note the smoke of the incense ascending with the saints' prayers before God. Gill notes the incense being put upon burning coals of fire caused a smoke to arise like a cloud, so that the whole temple was filled with it; so the prayers of the saints, perfumed, ascend before God. The witnesses note John seeing the smoke of the incense ascend with the prayers of the saints before God out of the angel's hand; the saints' prayers, perfumed and accepted, rising up before God — that John sees the prayers received: "the smoke of the incense, which came with the prayers of the saints, ascended up before God out of the angel's hand"; the witnesses note the picture of accepted prayer — as the incense, kindled on the coals, sends up its fragrant smoke in a cloud, so the prayers of the saints, joined with and perfumed by the incense of Christ's merit, "ascended up before God" (rose acceptably into his very presence) "out of the angel's hand" (presented by the great Intercessor); so that the believer's prayers, offered through Christ, do not fall to the ground but rise as sweet incense before the throne, received and answered by God; the smoke of the incense... ascended up before God out of the angel's hand. The witnesses note the smoke of the incense, which came with the prayers of the saints. The witnesses note ascended up before God out of the angel's hand.

The witnesses note the smoke of the incense ascending with the saints' prayers before God — the believer's prayers, perfumed by Christ's merit, rising acceptably into God's presence, not falling to the ground.

So the witnesses make the verse the saints' prayers ascending accepted before God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the prayers of the saints, perfumed by Christ's incense, rise acceptably before God's throne. The witnesses note the smoke of incense: "the smoke of the incense" — Gill notes the fragrant smoke rising; the smoke of incense; the the smoke; the fragrant; the rising; the a cloud. The expositors observe the with the prayers: "which came with the prayers of the saints" — joined with the saints' prayers; the with the prayers; the joined; the the saints; the perfumed; the the incense. The witnesses press the ascended before God: "ascended up before God" — rose acceptably into his presence; the ascended before God; the rose; the acceptably; the his presence; the received. The expositors draw the lesson on the out of the angel's hand: "out of the angel's hand" — presented by the Intercessor; the out of the angel's hand; the presented; the the Intercessor; the Christ; the offered. The witnesses observe the accepted prayer: the picture of accepted prayer; the accepted prayer; the received; the answered; the rises; the sweet. The expositors note the not fall to the ground: the prayers do not fall to the ground; the not fall to the ground; the do not fall; the rise; the through Christ; the received. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the smoke of the incense which came with the prayers of the saints, the ascended up before God out of the angel's hand, and the prayers ascending. The verse thus records the saints' prayers ascending accepted before God — teaching that the prayers of the saints, perfumed by Christ's incense, rise acceptably before his throne.
5:
"And the angel took the censer, and filled it with fire of the altar, and cast it into the earth: and there were voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake." The witnesses note the angel casting the altar-fire to the earth, with voices, thunders, and an earthquake. Barnes notes this is a new symbol, designed to furnish a new representation of future events; the same censer that offered prayer is now filled with fire and cast down in judgment. The witnesses note John seeing the angel fill the censer with fire of the altar and cast it to the earth, followed by voices, thunderings, lightnings, and an earthquake; the altar-fire cast down in judgment, answering the saints' prayers — that the same censer that bore up the prayers now becomes an instrument of judgment: "the angel took the censer, and filled it with fire of the altar, and cast it into the earth: and there were voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake"; the witnesses note the solemn connection — the "fire of the altar" (the very altar from which the prayers ascended) is now cast down upon the earth, and at once follow "voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake," the tokens of God's coming judgment; the witnesses note that this answers the martyrs' prayers (ch.6) and the prayers of all saints — God takes the fire from the altar of intercession and sends it forth as judgment on the earth; so that the prayers of the saints are not only accepted but become the very ground of the judgments now to be loosed by the trumpets; there were voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake. The witnesses note the angel took the censer, and filled it with fire of the altar, and cast it into the earth. The witnesses note there were voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake.

The witnesses note the angel casting the altar-fire to the earth, with voices, thunders, and an earthquake — the fire of intercession sent forth as judgment, answering the prayers of the saints.

So the witnesses make the verse the altar-fire cast down in judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that God takes the fire from the altar of intercession and sends it as judgment, answering the saints' prayers. The witnesses note the took the censer: "the angel took the censer" — Barnes notes a new symbol of judgment; the took the censer; the a new symbol; the judgment; the the same censer; the now. The expositors observe the fire of the altar: "filled it with fire of the altar" — the fire from the altar of prayer; the fire of the altar; the the fire; the the altar; the of prayer; the kindled. The witnesses press the cast into the earth: "and cast it into the earth" — cast down upon the earth; the cast into the earth; the cast down; the the earth; the judgment; the loosed. The expositors draw the lesson on the voices and thunders: "voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake" — tokens of coming judgment; the voices and thunders; the the tokens; the judgment; the lightnings; the earthquake. The witnesses observe the answers prayers: this answers the saints' prayers; the answers prayers; the the martyrs; the the saints; the answered; the the prayers. The expositors note the the ground of judgment: the prayers become the ground of the judgments; the the ground of judgment; the the prayers; the loosed; the the trumpets. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the there were voices and thunderings and lightnings and an earthquake, and the altar-fire cast down. The verse thus records the altar-fire cast down in judgment — teaching that God takes the fire from the altar of intercession and sends it as judgment, answering the saints' prayers.
6:
"And the seven angels which had the seven trumpets prepared themselves to sound." The witnesses note the seven angels making ready to sound their trumpets. Barnes notes the seven angels prepared themselves to sound, evidently in succession, perhaps by arranging themselves in the order in which they were to sound. The witnesses note John seeing the seven angels with the seven trumpets prepare themselves to sound; the angels making ready to sound the trumpets of judgment — that, the altar-fire being cast down, the seven angels now ready themselves for their solemn task: "the seven angels which had the seven trumpets prepared themselves to sound"; the witnesses note the orderly preparation — the angels, each holding his trumpet, set themselves in readiness, evidently to sound one after another, in succession, arranging themselves in the appointed order; the witnesses note the solemn pause of preparation before the first trumpet — all is now set for the great series of trumpet-judgments to begin, the angels poised and waiting on God's signal; so that the verse marks the threshold of the trumpets, the heavenly ministers prepared and about to sound forth the judgments that answer the prayers of the saints; the seven angels which had the seven trumpets prepared themselves to sound. The witnesses note the seven angels which had the seven trumpets. The witnesses note prepared themselves to sound.

The witnesses note the seven angels making ready to sound — each with his trumpet, set in order to sound in succession; the threshold of the trumpet-judgments, the angels poised and waiting on God's signal.

So the witnesses make the verse the angels preparing to sound the trumpets. The lesson the expositors draw is that the seven angels ready themselves to sound their trumpets in succession, on the threshold of the judgments. The witnesses note the seven angels: "the seven angels which had the seven trumpets" — the angels with their trumpets; the seven angels; the the trumpets; the each; the the angels; the armed. The expositors observe the prepared themselves: "prepared themselves to sound" — Barnes notes they made ready; the prepared themselves; the made ready; the to sound; the readiness; the set. The witnesses press the in succession: in succession, one after another; the in succession; the one after another; the in turn; the the order; the sound. The expositors draw the lesson on the the appointed order: arranging themselves in the appointed order; the the appointed order; the arranged; the the order; the set; the ready. The witnesses observe the the threshold: the threshold of the trumpet-judgments; the the threshold; the the trumpets; the the judgments; the about to begin; the poised. The expositors note the waiting on God: poised and waiting on God's signal; the waiting on God; the poised; the God's signal; the ready; the the trumpets. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the seven angels which had the seven trumpets, the prepared themselves to sound, and the angels making ready. The verse thus records the angels preparing to sound the trumpets — teaching that the seven angels ready themselves to sound in succession, on the threshold of the judgments.
7:
"The first angel sounded, and there followed hail and fire mingled with blood, and they were cast upon the earth: and the third part of trees was burnt up, and all green grass was burnt up." The witnesses note the first trumpet — hail and fire mingled with blood. Matthew Henry notes the first angel sounded and the events were dismal: a terrible storm of hail and fire mingled with blood, whether to be understood of literal or figurative judgments, fearful desolations. The witnesses note John seeing the first angel sound, and hail and fire mingled with blood cast on the earth, burning a third of the trees and all green grass; the first trumpet bringing a storm of fiery, bloody judgment on the earth — that at the first angel's sounding a terrible storm falls: "there followed hail and fire mingled with blood, and they were cast upon the earth: and the third part of trees was burnt up, and all green grass was burnt up"; the witnesses note the imagery of desolating judgment — "hail and fire mingled with blood," a fearful tempest, is cast down on the earth, and the effect is destruction by burning: "the third part of trees" and "all green grass" consumed; the witnesses note the interpretive variety — some (with the historicist commentators) read this of the desolating invasions that fell upon the Roman empire, others of literal or figurative storms of judgment; whichever the application, the first trumpet sounds forth a real and grievous judgment of God on a portion of the earth; the third part of trees was burnt up, and all green grass was burnt up. The witnesses note hail and fire mingled with blood... were cast upon the earth. The witnesses note the third part of trees was burnt up, and all green grass was burnt up.

The witnesses note the first trumpet — hail and fire mingled with blood cast on the earth, burning a third of the trees and all green grass; a desolating fiery judgment on a portion of the earth.

So the witnesses make the verse the first trumpet's fiery, bloody storm. The lesson the expositors draw is that the first trumpet brings a desolating judgment of hail and fire on the earth, burning trees and grass. The witnesses note the first angel sounded: "the first angel sounded" — Matthew Henry notes dismal events followed; the first angel sounded; the dismal; the the events; the the storm; the sounded. The expositors observe the hail and fire: "hail and fire mingled with blood" — a fearful tempest; the hail and fire; the with blood; the the tempest; the fearful; the cast down. The witnesses press the cast on the earth: "cast upon the earth" — cast down on the earth; the cast on the earth; the cast down; the the earth; the the storm; the falls. The expositors draw the lesson on the third of trees: "the third part of trees was burnt up" — a third of the trees consumed; the third of trees; the a third; the the trees; the burnt; the consumed. The witnesses observe the all grass: "and all green grass was burnt up" — all green grass burnt; the all grass; the green grass; the burnt; the consumed; the destroyed. The expositors present the readings: the witnesses note some read this of invasions on the Roman empire, others of literal or figurative storms of judgment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the hail and fire mingled with blood were cast upon the earth, the the third part of trees was burnt up and all green grass was burnt up, and the first trumpet. The verse thus records the first trumpet's fiery storm — teaching that the first trumpet brings a desolating judgment of hail and fire, burning trees and grass.
8:
"And the second angel sounded, and as it were a great mountain burning with fire was cast into the sea: and the third part of the sea became blood;" The witnesses note the second trumpet — a burning mountain cast into the sea. Barnes notes (in the historicist reading) this refers to the second of the great events that contributed to the downfall of the Roman empire; a great burning mass cast into the sea, turning a third of it to blood. The witnesses note John seeing the second angel sound, and a great burning mountain cast into the sea, a third of the sea becoming blood; the second trumpet bringing a fiery mass into the sea, turning it to blood — that at the second angel's sounding a vast burning mass falls into the sea: "as it were a great mountain burning with fire was cast into the sea: and the third part of the sea became blood"; the witnesses note the figure — "a great mountain burning with fire" (some huge, fiery mass, an image of a great destroying power or judgment) is hurled "into the sea," and the effect is that "the third part of the sea became blood," a fearful token of slaughter and death; the witnesses note the historicist application many give — that this figures some great power or invasion falling upon the maritime parts of the empire, with much bloodshed; whichever the reading, the second trumpet sounds a judgment of God upon the sea, a third part of it turned to blood; the third part of the sea became blood. The witnesses note as it were a great mountain burning with fire was cast into the sea. The witnesses note the third part of the sea became blood.

The witnesses note the second trumpet — a great burning mountain cast into the sea, a third of it turned to blood; an image of a great destroying power falling with much bloodshed upon the sea.

So the witnesses make the verse the second trumpet's burning mountain in the sea. The lesson the expositors draw is that the second trumpet brings a great fiery mass into the sea, turning a third of it to blood. The witnesses note the second angel sounded: "the second angel sounded" — the second trumpet; the second angel sounded; the the second; the the trumpet; the sounded; the in turn. The expositors observe the burning mountain: "a great mountain burning with fire" — a huge fiery mass; the burning mountain; the a fiery mass; the great; the destroying; the the power. The witnesses press the cast into the sea: "was cast into the sea" — hurled into the sea; the cast into the sea; the hurled; the the sea; the the judgment; the falls. The expositors draw the lesson on the third became blood: "the third part of the sea became blood" — a third turned to blood; the third became blood; the a third; the to blood; the slaughter; the death. The witnesses observe the the historicist reading: many read it of a power falling on the maritime empire; the the historicist reading; the a power; the the empire; the the sea; the bloodshed. The expositors note the judgment on the sea: the second trumpet a judgment on the sea; the judgment on the sea; the the sea; the a third; the to blood; the the trumpet. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as it were a great mountain burning with fire was cast into the sea, the and the third part of the sea became blood, and the second trumpet. The verse thus records the second trumpet's burning mountain — teaching that the second trumpet brings a great fiery mass into the sea, turning a third to blood.
9:
"And the third part of the creatures which were in the sea, and had life, died; and the third part of the ships were destroyed." The witnesses note the destruction of a third of the sea-creatures and ships. Gill notes (in the historicist reading) by the creatures in the sea men are meant, the inhabitants of the Roman empire; the loss of life and of ships figuring great destruction of people and commerce. The witnesses note John seeing a third of the living creatures in the sea die and a third of the ships destroyed; the further effect of the second trumpet — death and ruin in the sea — that the burning mountain's fall has a twofold destructive effect: "the third part of the creatures which were in the sea, and had life, died; and the third part of the ships were destroyed"; the witnesses note the completeness of the ruin within its bounds — not only is a third of the sea turned to blood, but "the third part of the creatures... which had life, died" (a great destruction of living things, taken by the commentators as figuring the death of multitudes of men) and "the third part of the ships were destroyed" (the ruin of commerce, trade, and wealth that the sea carries); the witnesses note again the historicist reading — figuring the loss of life and the destruction of the maritime power and trade of the empire under some great judgment; yet, as with all the trumpets, the destruction is limited to "the third part," bounded by God's hand; the third part of the ships were destroyed. The witnesses note the third part of the creatures which were in the sea, and had life, died. The witnesses note and the third part of the ships were destroyed.

The witnesses note the second trumpet's further ruin — a third of the sea-creatures dying and a third of the ships destroyed; the death of multitudes and the ruin of commerce, yet bounded to a third.

So the witnesses make the verse the death and ruin in the sea. The lesson the expositors draw is that the second trumpet destroys a third of the sea-creatures and ships, yet the ruin is bounded to a third. The witnesses note the creatures died: "the third part of the creatures which were in the sea... died" — Gill notes figuring the death of men; the creatures died; the a third; the the creatures; the died; the multitudes. The expositors observe the had life: "and had life" — the living things destroyed; the had life; the living things; the destroyed; the the creatures; the died. The witnesses press the death of multitudes: taken as figuring the death of multitudes of men; the death of multitudes; the of men; the the inhabitants; the the empire; the died. The expositors draw the lesson on the ships destroyed: "the third part of the ships were destroyed" — the ruin of commerce; the ships destroyed; the commerce; the trade; the wealth; the ruined. The witnesses observe the the historicist reading: figuring the loss of the maritime power and trade; the the historicist reading; the the maritime power; the the trade; the the empire; the ruined. The expositors note the bounded to a third: the destruction bounded to a third; the bounded to a third; the a third; the limited; the God's hand; the bounded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the third part of the creatures which were in the sea and had life died, the and the third part of the ships were destroyed, and the ruin in the sea. The verse thus records the death and ruin in the sea — teaching that the second trumpet destroys a third of the sea-creatures and ships, yet bounded to a third.
10:
"And the third angel sounded, and there fell a great star from heaven, burning as it were a lamp, and it fell upon the third part of the rivers, and upon the fountains of waters;" The witnesses note the third trumpet — a great burning star falling on the rivers and fountains. Barnes notes a star is a natural emblem of a prince or ruler; the falling of a great burning star indicates some important event in the downfall of the Roman empire, the fall of some leader. The witnesses note John seeing the third angel sound, and a great star burning like a lamp fall on a third of the rivers and the fountains of waters; the third trumpet casting a blazing star upon the springs and rivers — that at the third angel's sounding a great star falls: "there fell a great star from heaven, burning as it were a lamp, and it fell upon the third part of the rivers, and upon the fountains of waters"; the witnesses note the figure — a "great star," blazing "as it were a lamp," falls from heaven upon the fresh waters, "the rivers" and "the fountains of waters" (the springs of life-giving water); the witnesses note the reading many give — that a "star" figures a great person, leader, or teacher fallen from his high place, and his fall corrupts the very sources from which the people drink; whichever the application, the third trumpet sends its judgment not on sea but on the fresh waters, the rivers and springs, poisoning the sources of life; it fell upon the third part of the rivers, and upon the fountains of waters. The witnesses note there fell a great star from heaven, burning as it were a lamp. The witnesses note it fell upon the third part of the rivers, and upon the fountains of waters.

The witnesses note the third trumpet — a great burning star falling on the rivers and fountains; read as a great leader or teacher fallen from his place, his fall corrupting the very sources of life.

So the witnesses make the verse the third trumpet's blazing star on the waters. The lesson the expositors draw is that the third trumpet casts a burning star upon the rivers and springs, the sources of life-giving water. The witnesses note the third angel sounded: "the third angel sounded" — the third trumpet; the third angel sounded; the the third; the the trumpet; the sounded; the in turn. The expositors observe the great star: "a great star from heaven, burning as it were a lamp" — Barnes notes a star figures a ruler; the great star; the a ruler; the a prince; the blazing; the a lamp. The witnesses press the fell on the rivers: "it fell upon the third part of the rivers" — fell on the fresh waters; the fell on the rivers; the the fresh waters; the the rivers; the the springs; the fell. The expositors draw the lesson on the fountains: "and upon the fountains of waters" — the springs of life-giving water; the the fountains; the the springs; the life-giving; the the waters; the the sources. The witnesses observe the a leader fallen: a great leader or teacher fallen from his place; the a leader fallen; the a teacher; the fallen; the his place; the corrupts. The expositors note the corrupts the sources: his fall corrupts the sources of life; the corrupts the sources; the of life; the the springs. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it fell upon the third part of the rivers and upon the fountains of waters, and the third trumpet. The verse thus records the third trumpet's blazing star — teaching that the third trumpet casts a burning star upon the rivers and springs.
11:
"And the name of the star is called Wormwood: and the third part of the waters became wormwood; and many men died of the waters, because they were made bitter." The witnesses note the star named Wormwood embittering the waters and killing many. Gill notes the star is called Wormwood because of the bitter afflictions, sorrows, and distresses which it was the instrument of; as Naomi called herself Mara, because the Almighty had dealt bitterly with her. The witnesses note John recording that the star's name is Wormwood, that a third of the waters became wormwood, and many died of the bitter waters; the embittering of the waters by Wormwood, bringing death to many — that the fallen star is named and its deadly effect described: "the name of the star is called Wormwood: and the third part of the waters became wormwood; and many men died of the waters, because they were made bitter"; the witnesses note the meaning of the name — "Wormwood," a bitter and poisonous herb, names the star by its effect: it turns the sweet waters bitter, so that "many men died of the waters" that should have given them life; the witnesses note the figure of bitter affliction and corrupted teaching — whether read of bitter calamities or of corrupt doctrine poisoning the springs from which souls drink, the result is death to many; so that the third trumpet brings a judgment that embitters the sources of life and slays multitudes through the bitterness; many men died of the waters, because they were made bitter. The witnesses note the name of the star is called Wormwood. The witnesses note many men died of the waters, because they were made bitter.

The witnesses note the star Wormwood embittering a third of the waters, so that many died — the sweet waters turned bitter, whether bitter calamity or corrupt teaching poisoning the springs of life.

So the witnesses make the verse the embittering of the waters bringing death. The lesson the expositors draw is that Wormwood turns the sweet waters bitter, so that many die of the sources that should give life. The witnesses note the called Wormwood: "the name of the star is called Wormwood" — Gill notes named for the bitter afflictions it caused; the called Wormwood; the bitter; the afflictions; the the name; the the effect. The expositors observe the bitter herb: Wormwood, a bitter and poisonous herb; the bitter herb; the poisonous; the bitter; the the herb; the names. The witnesses press the waters became wormwood: "the third part of the waters became wormwood" — the sweet waters turned bitter; the waters became wormwood; the the sweet waters; the bitter; the a third; the corrupted. The expositors draw the lesson on the many died: "many men died of the waters" — many died of the bitter waters; the many died; the the waters; the bitter; the death; the slain. The witnesses observe the made bitter: "because they were made bitter" — the waters made bitter; the made bitter; the the waters; the bitter; the corrupted; the death. The expositors note the bitter calamity or doctrine: whether bitter calamity or corrupt teaching; the bitter calamity or doctrine; the calamity; the corrupt doctrine; the poisons; the death. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the name of the star is called Wormwood and the third part of the waters became wormwood, the many men died of the waters because they were made bitter, and the embittering. The verse thus records the embittering of the waters — teaching that Wormwood turns the sweet waters bitter, so that many die of the sources that should give life.
12:
"And the fourth angel sounded, and the third part of the sun was smitten, and the third part of the moon, and the third part of the stars; so as the third part of them was darkened, and the day shone not for a third part of it, and the night likewise." The witnesses note the fourth trumpet — the darkening of a third of sun, moon, and stars. Gill notes some think this refers to the darkness brought upon the doctrine of Christ's person by heresy; the great lights of heaven smitten, a third part darkened. The witnesses note John seeing the fourth angel sound, and a third of the sun, moon, and stars smitten and darkened, so that a third of day and night gave no light; the fourth trumpet darkening the great lights of heaven — that at the fourth angel's sounding the heavenly lights are struck: "the third part of the sun was smitten, and the third part of the moon, and the third part of the stars; so as the third part of them was darkened, and the day shone not for a third part of it, and the night likewise"; the witnesses note the figure — the "sun," "moon," and "stars," the great lights that rule the day and night, are "smitten" so that a third of their light fails, and a third of day and night is left in darkness; the witnesses note the reading the commentators give — that the darkening of the lights figures the obscuring of rulers, teachers, and the light of truth, whether by the fall of the empire's governors or by the darkening of gospel doctrine through heresy; whichever the application, the fourth trumpet brings a judgment of darkness, a partial eclipse of the light that should guide men; the day shone not for a third part of it, and the night likewise. The witnesses note the third part of the sun was smitten, and the third part of the moon, and the third part of the stars. The witnesses note the day shone not for a third part of it, and the night likewise.

The witnesses note the fourth trumpet darkening a third of sun, moon, and stars — the obscuring of rulers, teachers, and the light of truth; a judgment of darkness, a partial eclipse of guiding light.

So the witnesses make the verse the fourth trumpet's darkening of the lights. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fourth trumpet smites a third of the heavenly lights, figuring the obscuring of rulers and the light of truth. The witnesses note the fourth angel sounded: "the fourth angel sounded" — the fourth trumpet; the fourth angel sounded; the the fourth; the the trumpet; the sounded; the in turn. The expositors observe the third smitten: "the third part of the sun was smitten" — a third of the lights struck; the third smitten; the a third; the the sun; the the moon; the the stars. The witnesses press the darkened: "the third part of them was darkened" — a third of the light fails; the darkened; the a third; the the light; the fails; the obscured. The expositors draw the lesson on the day and night: "the day shone not for a third part of it, and the night likewise" — a third of day and night dark; the day and night; the a third; the no light. The expositors note the gospel darkened: or the darkening of gospel doctrine by heresy; the gospel darkened; the heresy; the the truth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the day shone not for a third part of it and the night likewise, and the fourth trumpet. The verse thus records the fourth trumpet's darkening — teaching that the fourth trumpet smites a third of the heavenly lights, figuring the obscuring of the light of truth.
13:
"And I beheld, and heard an angel flying through the midst of heaven, saying with a loud voice, Woe, woe, woe, to the inhabiters of the earth by reason of the other voices of the trumpet of the three angels, which are yet to sound!" The witnesses note the angel proclaiming threefold woe for the three trumpets yet to sound. Gill notes some copies and versions read "an eagle" instead of "an angel," which agrees with flying; the cry of woe announces the greater calamities of the trumpets still to come. The witnesses note John seeing an angel (or eagle) fly through heaven crying "Woe, woe, woe" to the earth-dwellers because of the three trumpets yet to sound; the solemn threefold woe announcing the worse judgments still to come — that, the first four trumpets being sounded, John hears a solemn warning of the worse to follow: "an angel flying through the midst of heaven, saying with a loud voice, Woe, woe, woe, to the inhabiters of the earth by reason of the other voices of the trumpet of the three angels, which are yet to sound"; the witnesses note the division this marks — the first four trumpets are now past, and the remaining three are set apart as "woe" trumpets, of a heavier and more dreadful kind; the threefold "Woe, woe, woe" answers to the three trumpets yet to sound, each bringing its own woe upon "the inhabiters of the earth" (the ungodly and unrepentant); so that this flying herald warns the world that the worst is yet to come, the trumpet-judgments rising to their dreadful climax in the three woes; Woe, woe, woe, to the inhabiters of the earth. The witnesses note an angel flying through the midst of heaven, saying with a loud voice, Woe, woe, woe, to the inhabiters of the earth. The witnesses note by reason of the other voices of the trumpet of the three angels, which are yet to sound.

The witnesses note the flying angel's threefold "Woe, woe, woe" — announcing the three trumpets yet to sound as heavier "woe" trumpets; the world warned that the worst judgments are yet to come.

So the witnesses make the verse the threefold woe announcing the worse trumpets. The lesson the expositors draw is that the last three trumpets are set apart as woe-trumpets, the flying herald warning the world the worst is yet to come. The witnesses note the angel flying: "an angel flying through the midst of heaven" — Gill notes some read "an eagle"; the angel flying; the an eagle; the through heaven; the flying; the the herald. The expositors observe the loud voice: "saying with a loud voice" — a loud, solemn cry; the loud voice; the loud; the solemn; the the cry; the warning. The witnesses press the threefold woe: "Woe, woe, woe" — three woes for three trumpets; the threefold woe; the three woes; the three trumpets; the heavier; the dreadful. The expositors draw the lesson on the inhabiters of the earth: "to the inhabiters of the earth" — the ungodly and unrepentant; the the ungodly; the unrepentant; the woe. The witnesses observe the the three yet to sound: "the three angels, which are yet to sound" — the three trumpets still to come; the the three yet to sound; the still to come; the the woes. The expositors note the worst yet to come: the world warned the worst is yet to come; the worst yet to come; the warned; the the climax. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the by reason of the other voices of the trumpet of the three angels which are yet to sound, and the threefold woe. The verse thus records the threefold woe announcing the worse trumpets — teaching that the last three trumpets are woe-trumpets, the herald warning the world the worst is yet to come.
